coredata.dc:title: Ceria/POMs hybrid nanoparticles as a mimicking metallopeptidase for treatment of neurotoxicity of amyloid-β peptide
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Yijia
Li, Meng
Dong, Kai
Gao, Nan
Ren, Jinsong
Zheng, Yongchen
Qu, Xiaogang
coredata.dc:description: Protein misfolding to amyloid aggregates is the hallmark for neurodegenerative disease. While much attention has been paid to screen natural proteases that can degrade amyloid-β peptides (Aβ), it is difficult to apply them in the clinics with the intractable problem of immunogenicity in living organisms. Herein, we rationally designed an artificial nanozyme, Ceria/Polyoxometalates hybrid (CeONP@POMs) with both proteolytic and superoxide dismutase (SOD) activities. Our results indicated that CeONP@POMs could efficiently degrade Aβ aggregates and reduce intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS). More importantly, CeONP@POMD could not only promote PC12 cell proliferation and can cross blood−brain barrier (BBB), but also inhibit Aβ-induced BV2 microglial cell activation which was demonstrated by immunoluorescence assay and flow cytometry measurements. In vivo studies further indicated that CeONP@POMD as nanozyme possessed good biocompatibility, evidenced by a detailed study of their biodistribution, body weight change, and in vivo toxicology. Therefore, our results pave the way for design of multifunctional artificial nanozyme for treatment of neurotoxicity of amyloid-β peptide.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alzheimer's disease
Amyloid-β peptides
Nanozyme
Hydrolytic activity
Polyoxometalates

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials Design and Medical Device Performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: BV2 cell culture and immunofluorescent staining
Transport across the BBB model in vitro
Hydrolytic effects of CeONP@POMD on Aβ
Clearance of ROS in PC12 cells
The effect of CeONP@POMD on essential biological protein
Effect of CeONP@POMD on Aβ-mediated microglial cell activation
Biodistribution pattern and toxicity of CeONP@POMD in animal model
Transport across the blood−brain barrier (BBB) in vitro and in vivo
The hydrolytic and SOD mechanism of CeONP@POMD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by 973 Project ( 2011CB936004 , 2012CB720602 ) and NSFC ( 21210002 , 21431007 , and 21533008 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic drawing of synthesis of CeONP@POMs. (B) TEM image of CeONP@POMD, (C) wide-angle XRD pattern of POMD, CeONP and CeONP@POMD. (D) XPS spectrum of tungsten in CeONP and CeONP@POMD.
The hydrolytic activity of CeONP@POMs. The time dependence of the absorbance at 410 nm and Lineweaver-Burk plot of the BAPNA hydrolysis catalyzed by (A) CeONP@POMD, (B) CeONP@POMK, (C) CeONP@POMA

(A) scheme of CeONP@POMD as hydrolytic enzyme to degrade Aβ40 monomers. (B) Aggregation kinetics of Aβ40 monomer was monitored by the development of ThT binding in the absent and present of CeONP@POMD, and the supernatant of pre-incubated Aβ40 and CeONP@POMD. The Aβ40 concentration was 50 μM, and the CeONP@POMD concentration was 100 μg/mL. The samples were measured in 10 mM HEPES (pH 7.3) after incubation at 37 °C for 7 days. The data points shown are the mean values ± standard error of the mean (SEM) from three independent experiments. (C) MALDI-TOF MS of Aβ40 monomers untreated or treated with CeONP@POMD. Details of the molecule weights were showed in Fig. S9.

(A) scheme of CeONP@POMD as a hydrolytic enzyme to degrade Aβ40 fibrils. (B) AFM images of Aβ40 fibrils untreated or treated with CeONP@POMD for 24 h. The scale bar is 600 nm. (C) TEM images of Aβ40 fibrils untreated or treated with CeONP@POMD for 24 h. The scale bar is 150 nm.

(A) MTT assay of PC12 cells treated with CeONP@POMD. (B) MTT assay of PC12 cells: 1) Control: Aβ40 untreated, Aβ40 with 2) 0, 3) 40, 4) 80, 5) 150 μg/mL CeONP@POMD. (C) Generation of ROS in PC12 cells. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001) SEM imagines of (D) ordinary PC12 cell, (E) PC12 cell treated with Aβ40, (F) PC12 cell treated with Aβ40 and CeONP@POMD. The data points shown are the mean values ± SEM from three independent experiments.

(A) Schematic representation of the cytotoxicity of Aβ40 and the effect of CeONP@POM on improving the process. SDS−PAGE analysis of (B) Aβ40 and (C) OVA treated or untreated with CeONP@POMD, (D) Aβ40 monomers treated or untreated with CeONP@POMD in CSF.

(A) The cellular morphology of the BV2 cells. (B) Images of CD11b positive cells, measured by direct immunoluorescence assay. (C) Overlap of (A) and (B). (D) Results of flow cytometry measurements of the ratios of CD11b positive cells. 1. control, 2. cells treated with Aβ40 for 24 h, 3. cells pretreated with 40 μg/mL CeONP@POMD and then Aβ40 for 24 h, and 4. cells pretreated with 80 μg/mL CeONP@POMD and then Aβ40 for 24 h. The normal cell morphology is indicated by black arrows, and the activated cell morphology is indicated by white arrows. Scale bar represents 50 μm. Each experiment was repeated three times.

(A) Schematic overview of the BCECs transwell assay for NPs to cross the BBB in vitro. (B) The relative transcytosis amount of CeONP@POMD, and CeONP@POMD under the blocking of endocytosis in vitro. CeONP@POMD nanoparticles were incubated with (C, D) BCECs and (E, F) macrophages (RAW-264.7) for 8 h (G, H) TEM images of blood capillary. After 5 min of brain perfusion in situ, the CeONP@POMD particles distributed in small clusters on the luminal plasma membrane. The white square of the left images was magnified into the right image. Arrows represent the position of the CeONP@POMD nanoparticles in cells.

head.ce:title: Ceria/POMs hybrid nanoparticles as a mimicking metallopeptidase for treatment of neurotoxicity of amyloid-β peptide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Chemical Biology, State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 1000039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology Central Laboratory, The Second Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology Central Laboratory
The Second Hospital of Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Protein misfolding to amyloid aggregates is the hallmark for neurodegenerative disease. While much attention has been paid to screen natural proteases that can degrade amyloid-β peptides (Aβ), it is difficult to apply them in the clinics with the intractable problem of immunogenicity in living organisms. Herein, we rationally designed an artificial nanozyme, Ceria/Polyoxometalates hybrid (CeONP@POMs) with both proteolytic and superoxide dismutase (SOD) activities. Our results indicated that CeONP@POMs could efficiently degrade Aβ aggregates and reduce intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS). More importantly, CeONP@POMD could not only promote PC12 cell proliferation and can cross blood−brain barrier (BBB), but also inhibit Aβ-induced BV2 microglial cell activation which was demonstrated by immunoluorescence assay and flow cytometry measurements. In vivo studies further indicated that CeONP@POMD as nanozyme possessed good biocompatibility, evidenced by a detailed study of their biodistribution, body weight change, and in vivo toxicology. Therefore, our results pave the way for design of multifunctional artificial nanozyme for treatment of neurotoxicity of amyloid-β peptide.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD), an intractable neurodegenerative disease, affects more than thirty million people worldwide, and 106 million people are estimated to suffer the disease by 2050 [1,2]. The key pathological markers in AD are cerebral extracellular amyloid plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles (NFT) [3], accompanied by local inflammation and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [4,5]. The plaques are composed mainly of 39–43 amino acid amyloid-β peptides (Aβ) that are generated via cleavage of the amyloid precursor protein (APP) by β- and γ-secretases [6]. Although the molecular mechanisms of AD pathogenesis remain unclear due to its complexity, recent advances have demonstrated that the onset of neurotoxicity is strongly associated with the aggregation process of monomer Aβ to oligomers and successively to fibrils in the pathogenesis [7,8]. Owing to the critical role of Aβ in AD pathogenesis, targeting the pathological properties of Aβ has been a promising therapeutic approach to treat AD [3,7]. Therefore, inhibiting β- and γ-secretases responsible to Aβ production [9], modulating Aβ aggregation [10,11] and increasing the rate of clearance of Aβ [12,13] have been used for AD treatments. Emerging evidence indicates that the steady-state levels of Aβ are determined by the balance between its production and degradation [14]. Recently, much attention has been paid to screen and design proteases that can degrade Aβ. Extracellular proteases, such as neprilysin [15], insulin-degrading enzyme [16], and endothelin converting enzyme [17], have been reported to regulate the concentration of secreted Aβ. Much effort has been devoted to regulate their activity by exogenous modulators [18]. However, it is difficult to apply them in the clinics with the intractable problem of immunogenicity in living organisms.

Instead, as a promising candidate for natural enzymes, catalytically active artificial enzymes possess obvious advantages compared to natural enzymes, such as controlled synthesis in low cost, tunability in catalytic activities, as well as high stability against stringent conditions. Recently, much attention has been paid to nanozymes, which are referred to nanoparticles with enzyme-like properties [19–21]. One nanozyme may offer many sites for catalysis, whereas pharmacological agents or enzymes offer only one active site per molecule [22]. Therefore, artificial nanozymes can have better catalytic activity. Moreover, rare earth metal-substituted polyoxometalates (POMs) have been reported to have the ability to hydrolyze peptides [23–25]. With this in mind, we attempt to design ceria/POMs nanozyme to clearance of the neurotoxicity of Aβ.

For better design, another feature for AD we have to take into account is that the patient's brain cells are suffered from abnormally high level of oxidative stress [26,27]. Prior literature [28,29] has reported that cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeONP) are in possession of antioxidant activity due to the synergistic activity between both Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions [30]. That is to say, CeONP may protect the brain against oxidative stress in AD. For this reason, we chose CeONP as a scaffold to design hydrolytic nanozyme.

Recently, our group reported that POMs, especially with Wells-Dawson structure, inhibited the aggregation of Aβ by binding to the His13 to Lys16 (HHQK) cationic region [31]. Herein, we rationally design a proteolytic enzyme by coating CeONP with POMs (CeONP@POMs). Since there are many kinds of POMs and it is unknown which type of POMs has the best effect, we screened a series of POMs with different structures, which were stable at physiological pH, to synthesize CeONP@POMs nanozyme for our initial studies. Furthermore, in consideration of the antioxidant activity of CeONP, the CeONP@POMs may act as a bifunctional agent for treatment of the neurotoxicity of Aβ. In vivo study demonstrated that our designed nanozyme had good biocompatibility and could cross BBB, evidenced by detailed studies of their biodistribution, in vivo toxicology and body weight. CeONP@POMs showed great potential for treatment of the neurotoxicity of Aβ.

Ce(NO3)3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Aβ1-40 was purchased from American Peptide (lot no. U10012). All other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as supplied. All chemicals were used as received from the suppliers without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ; Millipore Co., USA) was used throughout the experiment.

FI-TR analyses were carried out on a Bruker Vertex 70 FITR Spectrometer. X-ray measurements were performed on a Bruker D8 FOCUS Powder X-ray Diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation. Scanning transmission electron microscopy images were obtained with a Hitachi S-4800 FE-SEM. Ultraviolet–visible spectra were recorded with a JASCO-V550 spectrofluorometer. Transmission electron microscopy images were recorded using a FEI TECNAI G2 20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The zeta potential of the nanoparticles in water was measured in a Zetasizer 3000HS analyzer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a JASCO FP-6500 spectrofluorometer.

A solution containing 1 M cerium (III) nitrate (Aldrich, 99%) was added to 30.0 mL ammonium hydroxide (Sigma Aldrich, 30%) solution slowly, under continuous stirring. The mixture was then stirred continuously for 2 h, at this point the solution has changed from a deep brown color to a light yellow color indicating the formation of stabilized CeONP. The preparation was washed with distilled water by centrifuged at 4000 rpm for several times, until the pH of supernatant become neutral. Then CeONP resuspensed in distilled water. A solution containing POMs (10 mg/mL) was added to CeONP solution mentioned above. The mixture was then stirred continuously for 2 h. The samples were then centrifuged and washed three times with distilled water. Then CeONP@POMs resuspensed in distilled water.

Kinetic measurements were carried out by monitoring the absorbance change at 410 nm. Experiments were carried out using 75, 150, 300, 375 μg/mL CeONP@POMs in buffer solution (10 mM PBS, pH 4.0, 37 °C) with 1 mg/mL Nα-benzoyl-dl-arginine p-nitroanilide (BAPNA) as substrate. The Michaelis-Menten constant was calculated using the Lineweaver-Burk plot: 1/ν = (Km/Vmax)/C + 1/Vmax, where ν is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, and C is the concentration of substrate [33,34].

The SOD activity of CeONP@POMD were assayed by measuring the inhibition effect of photoreduction of nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT). The solutions contained riboflavin (20 μM), methionine (0.013 M), NBT (75 μM), and CeONP@POMD of various concentrations in 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.8). The mixtures were illuminated by a lamp with a constant light intensity for 5 min. After illumination, the absorbance was measured at 560 nm.

Aβ40 was purchased from American Peptide (lot no. U10012). Peptides were prepared as previously described [31]. Briefly, the powdered Aβ40 peptide was first dissolved in 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP) at a concentration of 1 mg/mL (about 240 μM). For further dissolution, the solution was shaken at 4 °C for 4 h in a 2 mL sealed vial. Then a stock solution was stored at −20 °C. Under a gentle stream of nitrogen, the polar solvent HFIP was removed by evaporation before experiment. Then Aβ40 peptide was dissolved in 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4, containing 150 mM NaCl). For the preparation of fibrils, Aβ40 solutions was incubated at 37 °C for 7 days as described earlier [35].

Aβ40 peptide (50 μM) in the absence or presence of CeONP@POMD was incubated at 37 °C for 2 days. Samples (10 μL) were analyzed by 12% SDS PAGE. Gels were run in a Tris/glycine system at 100 V for about 1 h. Finally, the gels were developed by the silver-stain method [31].

Aβ40 fibrils (50 μM) in the absence or presence of CeONP@POMD (100 μg/ml) were incubated for 2 days at 37 °C. Samples were dropped onto carbon-coated copper grids and incubated for 30 min. The grids were then blotted with filter paper to remove excess buffer. The substrates were rinsed with deionized water for 3 times. The samples were analyzed on a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-1011) operating at a voltage of 200 kV.

PC12 cells were seeded on 24-well plates with glass cover slips (10 mm in diameter) for 24 h before treated with Aβ40. After treated or untreated with CeONP@POMD, the PC12 cells were fixed with 2% glutaraldehyde and 2% paraformaldehyde in 0.01 M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) for 30 min at 4 °C. In order to dehydrate, the cells were treated in an ascending series of ethanol (50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, and 100%). Finally, cells were immersed in hexamethyldizilasane (HMDS) and dried. Cell morphology were monitored by SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BV2 cell culture and immunofluorescent staining
ce:section.ce:para: BV2 cells, obtained from American Type Culture Collection, were grown on plates in MEM media containing 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% serum l-glutamate and 1% streptomycin until 80% confluency. After incubation cells were transferred to a clean 24-well plate, fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin for 30 min and rinsed 3 times in PBS for 5 min. Cells were blocked in 0.1% triton-X and 10% BSA in PBS for 1 h and then incubated in primary antibody CD11b 1:500 (Rat polyclonal) in PBS overnight at 4 °C. Cells were rinsed 3 times in PBS for 5 min before incubation in secondary goat anti-rat (495 nm) at a 1:20,000 dilution of for 1 h. Glass coverslips were rinsed 3 times in PBS prior to adhesion to microscope slides. Fluorescence viewed with an Olympus BX-51 optical system microscope (Tokyo, Japan). Pictures were taken with an Olympus digital camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transport across the BBB model in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: For establishing the BBB in vitro model, which was used to study the BBB permeability of nanoparticles by Fang et al. [36,37], BCECs were seeded on polycarbonate 24-well transwell membranes of 1.0 μm mean pore size, 0.33 cm2 surface areas (FALCON Cell Culture Insert, Becton Dickinson Labware, USA). The integrity of cell monolayer was monitored using an epithelial voltohmmeter (Millicell-RES, Millipore, USA), and the Transendothelial Electrical Resistance (TEER) values above 200 Ω cm2 were selected for the experiments. CeONP@POMD was introduced into the apical side chamber. The basolateral medium and the filter membrane with the cells were collected 18 h after incubation. A blocking study was carried out by adding dynasore to the apical side chamber in advanced at a concentration of 80 μM followed by above-mentioned steps.

The test animals were S4880202 normal mice. All animal studies were conducted in accordance with the principles and procedures outlined in “Regulations for the Administration of Affairs Concerning Laboratory Animals”, approved by the National Council of China on October 31, 1988, and “The National Regulation of China for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals”, promulgated by the National Science and Technology Commission of China, on November 14, 1988 as Decree No. 2. Protocols were approved by the Committee of Jilin University Institutional Animal Care and Use. CeONP@POMD in saline was intravenously administered to mice through tail vain injection at 25 mg/kg body weight. The tissues (heart, spleen, liver, lung, kidney and brain) were collected from the control and test groups after 24 h and were immersed in a buffered solution of 4% paraformaldehyde for 24 h. The specimen was sectioned serially at 4 µm thickness and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The histological sections were observed under an optical microscope. To study the tissue distribution for CeONP@POMD nanoparticles, the organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidneys and brain) were surgically removed at various times after injection of CeONP@POMD through the tail vein. The blood in brain is removed by brain perfusion. The organs were placed into a centrifuge tube containing 4 mL of aqua regia, and then incubated for 4 h under heat treatment (80° C) for dissolution of the tissues. The obtained liquid was subjected to ICP-MS analysis (ICP-MS, Varian 720-ES).

We screened a series of POMs to synthesize nanozyme, including POMs with Wells-Dawson structure (K6[P2W18O62]; POMD), Keggin structure (K8[β-SiW11O39]; POMK) and Anderson structure (Na5[IMo6O24]; POMA), which are stable at physiological pH. CeONP was synthesized according to the previous reports [28,30] and characterized by TEM with a size approximately 3 nm (Fig. S1). POMs can act as a ligand coordinated to Ce ion, which has been determined by Parac-Vogt et [24]. Coordination of POMs oxygen atoms occupies a part of coordination sites around Ce ion on the surface of CeONP to form CeONP@POMs (Fig. 1 A). CeONP@POMs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). According to TEM image (Fig. 1B and Fig. S2), the CeONP@POMs nanoparticles dispersed well with a size about 5 nm. The size of CeONP@POMD is bigger than that of CeONP, indicating that CeONP was coated with POMs. Since POMs had similar structure, we took CeONP@POMD as an example to illustrate. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of POMD, CeONP and CeONP@POMD were shown in Fig. 1C. Although the XRD pattern demonstrated that pure Dawson salts had ordered and defined structure, typical peaks of CeONP@POMD were broadening, which were similar to CeONP. The coordination grafting of POMD onto CeONP was further evidenced by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Fig. S3). Peaks ranging from 600 to 1100 cm−1 corresponding to the Dawson structural vibrations, were distinguished clearly. 908 and 773 cm−1 band ascribed to νas chain vibrations in which WO, WOW, respectively [11]. The FT-IR bands at about 690 cm−1 corresponding to the CeO groups as were seen in CeONP and CeONP@POMD [38]. Since CeONP were coated by POMD, the peaks of CeONP from 1000 to 2000 cm−1 were very weak in CeONP@POMD, which were replaced by the peaks of POMD. FT-IR spectra of CeONP@POMD also indicated that the POMD was coated on CeONP. XPS surveys of CeONP and CeONP@POMD were also measured and shown in Fig. 1D. The W4f peaks became detectable just when POMD were coated to CeONP. As shown by the W4f peaks in XPS spectra, binding energies of about 34.8, 35.7 and 36.1 eV were assigned to tungsten atoms for W5+-states of oxide, W6+-states of oxide and hydroxide, respectively [39]. The XPS results (Fig. S4) further confirmed the successfully grafting of POMD onto CeONP. What's more, mixed valence states of cerium of CeONP and CeONP@POMD were verified by XPS (Fig. S4A), which suggested their potential superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity. To depict the electronic transitions in Ce3+ and Ce4+, XPS spectra of the CeONP@POMD was resolved into up to ten features grouped as u and v lines, respectively (Fig. S4B). The concentration of Ce3+ was calculated using the equation
(1)

C
e
3 +
]

A
u 0
+
A
u 1

A
u 0 ′

+
A

+
A
u 1 ′
+
A
v 0
+
A

+
A
v 2
+
A

+
A
v 1 ′
+
A
v 2 ′
where A
i is the integrated area of peak i.

The Ce3+ concentration in CeONP@POMD was found to be about 36%. Self et al. [30] have demonstrated that CeONP reveal SOD activity when the concentration of Ce3+ ranges from 40% to 22%, and CeONP containing high amounts of Ce(IV) is ineffective in SOD mimetic assays. Therefore, the XPS results suggested potential SOD activity of CeONP and CeONP@POMD. According to inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), the amount of POMD modified on CeONP was approximately 15.90%, covering about 24.9% of the surface of CeONP.

One issue that needs to be addressed is the stability of CeONP@POMs under physiological conditions. Thus, the zeta-potential and hydrodynamic diameter of CeONP@POMD were measured in water, PBS, DMEM supplemented with or without 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Fig. S5). The zeta-potential of CeONP@POMs remained extremely negative under the condition mentioned above. According to hydrodynamic diameter of CeONP@POMs, CeONP@POMs do not aggregate under physiological condition. These results indicated that CeONP@POMs were stable under physiological condition.

Nα-benzoyl-dl-arginine p-nitroanilide (BAPNA), which is a widely used substrate in studying the activity of proteolytic enzyme, was used as a chromogenic substrate to measure the proteolytic activity of these nanozymes. The proteolytic activities of CeONP, free POMD, CeONP@POMD, and the mixture of CeONP and free POMD were detected (Fig. S6). The results indicated that only CeONP@POMD performed proteolytic activity. The specific activity of CeONP@POMD was 64.71 U/mg (1 U = 1 μmol/min). Moreover, the kinetic parameters of CeONP@POMs were estimated by Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. 2 ), and summarized in Table S1. The Km is a measure of the binding affinity between nanoenzymes and substrates and kcat reveals catalytic activity [40]. These findings indicated that CeONP@POMD possessed proteolytic activity.

As shown in Fig. 2 and Table S1, the highest proteolytic activity was observed for CeONP@POMD. CeONP@POMK performed moderate hydrolytic activity. Smaller POMs with Anderson structure coated CeONP showed lowest activity (Figure S7). To gain a better understanding of the proteolytic properties of these nanozymes, the zeta potential was measured. The zeta potentials of CeONP@POMD, CeONP@POMK and CeONP@POMA were −48.2, −34.7 and −33.8 mV, respectively. So we assumed that surface charge of these nanozymes may be an important factor in hydrolytic activity. Although the zeta potentials of CeONP@POMK and CeONP@POMA were similar, their hydrolytic activities were different. So, the transition metal of POMK and POMA might be responsible for the hydrolytic activities, which were tungsten (W) and molybdenum (Mo), respectively. The electronegativity of W and Mo were 1.7 and 1.8 [41], so the reactivity of POMK was stronger than POMA. Since CeONP@POMD showed the highest hydrolytic activity, we took it as an example to study the properties of CeONP@POMs nanozyme in the following investigation.

In addition, the SOD activity of CeONP@POMD was quantified using a modified NBT assay system [31,35]. The NBT assay was based on the capacity of CeONP@POMD to inhibit the reduction of NBT by photochemically generated superoxide anion radical in the presence of riboflavin. As shown in Fig. S8, CeONP@POMD displayed SOD activity in a dose dependant manner because of the different valence state of Ce ion [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrolytic effects of CeONP@POMD on Aβ
ce:section.ce:para: The aggregation of Aβ40 is associated with the neurotoxicity in AD. Therefore, it is important to keep the balance of Aβ40 between its production and catabolism. To investigate the hydrolytic effect of CeONP@POMD on Aβ40 monomers, the hydrolysate was monitored by Matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS). After co-incubation of CeONP@POMD and Aβ40 monomers at 37 °C for 24 h, the unique peak of the Aβ40 monomers decreases and several low molecular weight peaks appear (Fig. 3 C and Fig. S9, details were showed in Fig. S9).

For further investigating the hydrolytic effect of CeONP@POMD on Aβ40 monomers, a standard thioflavin T (ThT) binding and sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) assay were performed. After incubating fresh Aβ40 alone at 37 °C, ThT fluorescence showed a sigmoidal shape as a function of incubation time, which was in agreement with the nucleation-dependent polymerization model. When Aβ40 monomers were incubated with CeONP@POMD, ThT fluorescence showed no increase, indicating lack of fibril formation. The supernatant, prepared by pre-incubated Aβ40 and CeONP@POMD at 37 °C, was continuously incubated at 37 °C for 7 days (Fig. 3B). The ThT fluorescence did not increase, indicating that the Aβ hydrolysates could not aggregate anymore. In control experiments, CeONP or CeONP@POMD, did not influence ThT fluorescence under our experimental conditions (Fig. S10). As shown in the SDS-PAGE (Fig. S11, lane 2), after 6 h of incubation with CeONP@POMD, the band of Aβ40 monomers became weaker, showing that Aβ40 monomers had been degraded. Even after treated by CeONP@POMD for 12 h (Fig. S11, lane 3), the monomers band nearly disappeared.

Moreover, we also investigated the hydrolytic effect of Aβ40 fibril by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and TEM. The Aβ40 fibrils were non-branched, helical filaments with diameters of approximate 10 nm and lengths of up to several micrometers. By contrast, when the preformed fibrils were incubated with CeONP@POMD for 24 h, it was hardly to find any large species (Fig. 4 B). After incubation for just 4 h, the CeONP@POMD were found closed to the end of fibrils (Fig. 4C), indicating that CeONP@POMD might adhere to long fibrils and then cleaved them. The possible mechanisms by which CeONP@POMD could cleave Aβ fibril can be described as follows. Our TEM results clearly showed that CeONP@POMD could bind to the Aβ fibril. This binding would lower the stability of the fibril and disaggregate the fibril to get oligomers or monomers. And then Aβ monomer can be hydrolyzed by CeONP@POMD, which further shift the equilibrium away from fibrillization.

With the activities of proteolysis and SOD, CeONP@POMD may block Aβ-mediated toxicity. We chose PC12 cell as a nerve cell model to explore their cellular metabolism and morphology by the influence of CeONP@POMD. To investigate the biocompatibility of CeONP@POMD, we performed MTT assay to probe cellular metabolism. CeONP@POMD showed significantly increased PC12 proliferation (Fig. 5 A), which was in concert with previous reports that CeONP can increase the proliferation of cells [42]. In control experiments, CeONP@POMD did not interact with MTT under our experimental conditions (Fig. S12). Fig. 5B showed that CeONP@POMD could effectively inhibit Aβ-mediated cytotoxicity. The morphology of PC12 cells were further studied by SEM. Compared with Aβ40 untreated cells (Fig. 5D), the morphology of PC12 cells became deformed (Fig. 5E) after treatment of Aβ40 fibrils. Evidently, CeONP@POMD was beneficial to the cells (Fig. 5F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clearance of ROS in PC12 cells
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the involvement of ROS in AD pathogenesis has influenced the presence of oxidative modifications to proteins, lipids, DNA, and RNA in the cortex [26,43,44]. Therefore, ROS generated through the process of Aβ aggregation has been a toxic factor. We detected the level of intracellular ROS by flow cytometry using dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as a probe [45]. Exposure of the PC12 cells to Aβ caused an increase in content of ROS (Fig. 5C). Pretreatment of the PC12 cells with CeONP diminished the levels of ROS by 35%, compared with the cells exposed to Aβ40 alone. Although CeONP could scavenge ROS, Aβ still existed and continued to generate ROS through the process of aggregation. By contrast, when the cells were treated with CeONP@POMD, the level of ROS decreased 68%. These results demonstrated that the antioxygenation of CeONP@POMD was more effective than CeONP because CeONP@POMD inhibited Aβ aggregation and ROS generation (Fig. 6 A). In control experiments, CeONP and CeONP@POMD alone, did not influence DCFH fluorescence under our experimental conditions (Fig. S13). CeONP@POMD could be an effective free-radical scavenger for the treatment of AD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CeONP@POMD on essential biological protein
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the influence of CeONP@POMD on the essential biological protein, we chose ovalbumin (OVA; pI 4.5) as an example of normal protein. As shown in Fig. 6B and C, CeONP@POMD would hydrolyze Aβ40 effectively rather than OVA. Although both Aβ40 and OVA carry negative charges at pH 7.4, there is cationic cluster HHQK on Aβ40 which can bind to POMD [49].

Aβ40 concentration in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is a diagnostic and therapeutic target for AD, so it is important to perform the experiment in CSF to check whether the nanozyme can hydrolyze Aβ40 monomers in a biological matrix. As shown in Fig. 6D, CeONP@POMD in mice CSF could decrease the concentration of Aβ40 significantly. Compared with other artificial proteases against AD [50], our bifunctional nanozyme could not only decrease the concentration of Aβ and ROS, but also benefit the injury cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CeONP@POMD on Aβ-mediated microglial cell activation
ce:section.ce:para: Aβ deposition-meadiated microglial cell activation is strongly correlated with the pathogenesis of AD [46]. Microglial cells, the most important immune cells in the central nervous system (CNS), have similar functions to macrophages in the brain [46]. The activation of microglial cells at early stages is benefit to nervous system repair via phagocytosis and releasing neurotrophic factors. However, excessive and prolonged activation of microglial cells can lead to a variety of pathological damages in the CNS. Therefore, regulation of microglial cell activation can be an effective way to prevent and treat AD. CD11b is a microglial surface integrin expressed solely during activation, and it has been indicated that disruption to homeostasis induces an “amoeboid” retraction of ramified processes, an enlarged cytoplasm, which is an indication of the activated state [46]. As shown in Fig. 7 , CeONP@POMD could protect the microglia from the Aβ-activated morphological changes. After activated by Aβ for 24 h, the pseudopodium of BV2 cells retract like amoebas (Fig. 7A). Preincubation of CeONP@POMD for 1 h, homeostasis of BV2 cells was not disrupted by Aβ. According to direct immumofluorescence method (Fig. 7B), the expression of CD11b was suppressed by CeONP@POMD, and was quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometry (Fig. 7D). Both direct immumofluorescence and flow cytometry indicated that CeONP@POMD could protect the microglia from the Aβ-mediated microglial cell activation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution pattern and toxicity of CeONP@POMD in animal model
ce:section.ce:para: The biodistribution of CeONP@POMD in the various mouse tissues, including heart, lungs, liver, kidney, spleen and brain was investigated through ICP-MS analysis of cerium and tungsten because of these two elements with very low background in the body (Fig. S14). The results demonstrated that CeONP@POMD underwent rapid and wide biodistribution to tissues within the time course examined. The highest concentration was found in liver, followed by lung, spleen, kidney and the lowest level in heart and brain. We could observe a gradual accumulation in the liver within 24 h. And markedly decreasing signals of Ce ion contents in all organs were observed after 48 h post-injection. The stepwise clearance of CeONP@POMD nanoparticles from the body suggested that the nanoparticles were primarily removed through a hepatobiliary/fecal route.

To determine whether the nanoparticles caused any harmful effect during its retention in the body, we investigated the toxicity through histological assessment. As shown in Fig. S15, no tissue damage or any other adverse effects associated with the administration of CeONP@POMD nanoparticles were observed compared with those of the control group after hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain. Furthermore, mouse health was closely monitored for changes to body weight. By appearance, mice administered with CeONP@POMD nanoparticles were normal and did not remarkablely drop in body weight compared with control animals injected with phosphate-buffered saline (Fig. S16). All of the above results indicated that CeONP@POMD nanoparticles showed good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transport across the blood−brain barrier (BBB) in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: For potential in vivo applications, CeONP@POMD should cross the BBB. Transport across the BBB of CeONP@POMD was measured by using transwell filters seeded with a compact brain capillary endothelial cells (BCECs) monolayer (Fig. 8 A) according to the previously reported method [36,37]. To evaluate the BBB-crossing efficacy of CeONP@POMD, the tungsten contents on the apical side (AS), cellular monolayer (CM), and basolateral side (BS) were analyzed by ICP-MS. Fig. 8B revealed that 4.4 ± 0.46% of the CeONP@POMD nanoparticles were found on the basolateral side. Additionally, about 0.83 ± 0.49% of the total added nanoparticles remained cell-uptaken. Transcytosis by the endocytic pathway was further demonstrated by a blocking study. Dynasore acts as a potent inhibitor of endocytic pathways by blocking vesicle formation within seconds of dynasore addition. Dynasore was preincubated in the apical medium on the BBB cellular model, which resulted in significantly reduced transport ratio of CeONP@POMD. Only 1.1 ± 0.38% of the CeONP@POMD nanoparticles was found on the basolateral side with preincubation of dynasore. As shown in Fig. 8C and D, CeONP@POMD were localized in the vesicle due to endocytosis of BCECs. Together, these data strongly demonstrated that the nanozymes could penetrate the BBB by the endocytic pathway as other nanoparticles [47].

Moreover, nanoparticles can also cross the BBB by loading to macrophages. Among various cell types, macrophages act as cellular/biological vehicles for transmitting nanoparticles to treat central nervous system (CNS) pathologies [48]. On account of their natural function of phagocytosis of foreign bodies, macrophages are well-suited for loading a variety of nanoparticles. In addition, macrophages are attracted to and infiltrate into the brain during inflammation. Fig. 8E and F showed that macrophages cells internalized CeONP@POMD nanoparticles by pinocytosis. Therefore, CeONP@POMD-loaded macrophages cells can actively cross a relatively intact BBB in response to inflammation, which is always accompanied with AD.

To further demonstrate the capability of CeONP@POMD in transporting across the BBB in vivo, ICP-MS was used to analyze the levels of tungsten after intravenous injection. A significant level of tungsten was found at the CeONP@POMD treated mouse brain compared with the control group. The efficiency of CeONP@POMD crossed BBB was about 0.54%. TEM images of blood capillary can also prove that CeONP@POMD are transported across the BBB. After 5 min of brain perfusion in situ, the CeONP@POMD particles distributed in small clusters on the luminal plasma membrane as showed in Fig. 8G and H.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The hydrolytic and SOD mechanism of CeONP@POMD
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrolytic and SOD activities of CeONP@POMD could be explained as follows. Firstly, a possible hydrolytic mechanism based on metallopeptidases was described as below (Fig. S17) [32]. Ce ion interacted with the carbonyl group of the proximal amide bond, which resulted in its polarization and made the carbon atom more susceptible to nucleophilic attack by water molecules [51]. Reactivity originated from the Lewis acidity of the Ce ion, which was brought into proximity with the peptide bond by electrostatic interactions of the POMs moiety with the protein surface. Water, which had an active role, could be delivered from the solvent (external delivery mechanism) or originate as a ligand coordinated to the metal ion (internal delivery mechanism). Secondly, the SOD activity were mainly caused by CeONP due to the synergistic activity between both Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions [30].

In summary, we designed and constructed a bifunctional artificial nanozyme with both proteolytic and SOD activities for the treatment of neurotoxicity of Aβ. Through screening different structures of POMs, CeONP@POMD with Wells-Dawson structure has the highest proteolytic activity to Aβ. Moreover, CeONP@POMD can effectively attenuate Aβ-mediated toxicity by clearance of Aβ and intracellular ROS. Compared with natural Aβ proteolytic enzymes, the nanozymes can overcome the drawbacks of natural proteases which can become invalid easily in complicated systems and hardly can be applied to clinic. Furthermore, the CeONP@POMD benefits cell proliferation and may promote the recovery of injury zone. Our designed artificial nanozyme can effectively inhibit Aβ aggregation and decrease cellular ROS. More importantly, it can cross BBB, regulate microglial cells and protect neuron cells from Aβ-related cytotoxicity. Therefore, this work would promote design of multifunctional artificial nanozyme for treatment of multifaceted neurotoxicity of amyloid-β peptide.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by 973 Project (2011CB936004, 2012CB720602) and NSFC (21210002, 21431007, and 21533008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2016.05.005.
